case_title: IDG BETEL, LLC, A CALIFORNIA LIMITED LIABILITY ET AL VS. MHDC CA MGP III, LLC, A CALIFORNIA LIMITED ET AL

docket_entries.description: LAW AND MOTION 302, DEC-05-2024 RE: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. NO APPEARANCE WAS MADE BY ANY PARTY, COUNSEL, OR INTERESTED PERSON. DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Dec-05-2024 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: RE: DEFENDANT MHDC CA MGP III, LLC DEMURRER to 1ST Amended COMPLAINT.

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel, or Interested Person.

The court adopts its tentative ruling. Defendant MHDC CA MGP III, LLC's demurrer to the first amended complaint (FAC) is overruled.

Plaintiffs and MHDC are general partners of the Betel and Esperanza-Colosimo partnerships, which develop and operate low-income and senior housing in San Francisco. MHDC allegedly misused partnership property and loaned money for non-partnership purposes, incurred liabilities and expenses without approval, comingled funds with the funds of other MHDC entities, improperly funded capital improvements without tapping into replacement reserves, and failed to make available books and records. (FAC para. 63, 68.) MHDC now demurs to the third through eighth causes of action (COA) in the FAC.

MHDC argues the action is derivative in nature, and plaintiffs lack standing because they have not alleged demand presentment or futility. An action is derivative if "the gravamen of the complaint is injury to the corporation," e.g., corporate mismanagement that causes overall harm to the entity. (Bader v. Anderson (2009) 179 Cal.App.4th 775, 788.) A direct claim, however, "enforce[s] a right against the corporation which the stockholder possesses as an individual." (Jones v. H.F. Ahmanson & Co. (1969) 1 Cal.3d 93, 107.) The individual wrong alleged need not be unique to that plaintiff, so long as the injury is not incidental to the corporation's injury. (Id.) "[T]he two actions are mutually exclusive: i.e., the right of action and recovery belongs either to the shareholders (direct action) or to the corporation (derivative action). [Citation.]" (Bader, supra, 179 Cal.App.4th at 793.)

Here, the gravamen of the complaint is direct. Plaintiffs do not allege corporate mismanagement harmed the partnerships. Rather, they allege MHDC acted to increase its value in the partnerships while decreasing that of plaintiffs. For instance, MHDC allegedly funded capital improvements with operating cash instead of replacement reserves, thus decreasing plaintiffs' distributions. (FAC para. 44.) MHDC points to the alleged misappropriation of $155k, and comingling of funds and property, as evidence of corporate injury. (Mem. p.7-8.) But this money was allegedly returned, and plaintiffs do not allege temporary loss of funds or property injured the partnerships. Thus, plaintiffs adequately plead direct claims. (See Everest Investors 8 v. McNeil Partners (2003) 114 Cal.App.4th 411, 429 [direct claim where merger harmed plaintiff by undervaluing its interest without harming the partnership]; Jara v. Suprema Meats, Inc. (2004) 121 Cal.App.4th 1238, 1257 [direct claim where majority stockholders' breach of fiduciary duty to minority stockholder resulted in the former retaining disproportionate share of corporation's value].)

Further, MHDC's authorities are unavailing. Wallner v. Parry Professional Bldg., Ltd. (1994) 22 Cal.App.4th 1446, 1250, addressed whether a limited partner's derivative action was allowed under a now-repealed statute, not whether the action was derivative. The claims in Bader, supra, 179 Cal.App.4th at 801, were derivative because alleged mismanagement injured the corporation, i.e., nondeductable bonuses and stocks. Here instead, MHDC's conduct was allegedly neutral to the partnerships but benefited MHDC and harmed plaintiffs. Similarly, the claims in Schuster v. Gardner (2005) 127 Cal.App.4th 305, 313, were derivative because the alleged corporate mismanagement (e.g., improperly recognizing revenue and understating expenses) primarily injured the corporation and diminished its stock value. Here instead, the primary injury is to plaintiffs, and no overall diminution in partnership value is alleged.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer. Clerk: Robert Woods. Not Reported. (302/RBU)

***After hearing, an order is signed.
